# AI Data Center Design Space

AI inference starts with a physical constraint: every token consumes power,
cooling, network capacity, memory bandwidth, accelerator time, and operational
attention.

## Chapter Job

Build the vocabulary required to compare data center choices for inference
systems.

## Design Axes

| Axis | Design choice | Inference consequence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Facility | Colocation, hyperscale region, managed GPU cloud | Control, speed, procurement risk |
| Power | Rack density, redundancy, power capping | Maximum accelerator density and burst behavior |
| Cooling | Air, rear-door heat exchangers, direct liquid, immersion | Rack density, reliability, maintenance model |
| Cluster | Single node, rack-scale, pod-scale, multi-region | Scheduling, fault domains, data movement |
| Network | Ethernet, InfiniBand, proprietary fabric | Collective performance and tail latency |
| Storage | Local NVMe, shared filesystem, object store | Model load time and checkpoint movement |
| Operations | Bare metal, Kubernetes, managed inference platform | Control surface and team burden |

## Compare/Contrast Pattern

For every data center design choice, ask:

- What workload shape does this favor?
- What failure mode does it create?
- What operational skill does it require?
- What measurement proves it works?
- What gets harder when the cluster grows?

## What This Enables Later

The rest of the book repeatedly returns to this chapter. Batching, KV cache,
tensor parallelism, disaggregated prefill/decode, and specialized systems only
make sense when the physical envelope is visible.
